A multimedia documentation of our visit to Fort Santiago and Rizal Park —
retracing the final steps of the Philippines' national hero.

COLLAGE · images/collage.jpg

Footprints of Manila's Heroes — Field Visitation Documentation

Watch · Our Documentary

Reliving the Life of Rizal

A visual journey through Fort Santiago and Rizal Park

✦
"To foretell the destiny of a nation, it is necessary to open the book that tells of her past." — José Rizal

This website documents our group's immersive visit to Fort Santiago, Intramuros, and Rizal Park — two of the most historically significant sites in the Philippines. Through photographs, written reflections, and critical analysis, we retrace the last days of Dr. José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda: his imprisonment, his trial, and his execution on December 30, 1896.

Using the ACR Framework — Appreciate, Critique, Recommend — we explore his life not only as an act of historical remembrance, but as a lens for understanding our responsibilities as young Filipinos today.

· · ·

Reliving Rizal: A Journey Through History

Presented by Illustrados  ·  BSPT 1–1
Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila  ·  Academic Year 2025–2026
Life and Works of Rizal · Final Term Project

In honor of José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda · December 30, 1896

Field Documentation

About the Project

✦

A visit to Fort Santiago, Intramuros, Manila — retracing the imprisonment and execution of José Rizal

I. Introduction

The site of Fort Santiago can be considered the most historically important place in the country. Constructed in 1571 as a military fort, it has been used not only for defense purposes but also as a detention facility for people who posed threats to the colonial government. The place has become an embodiment of oppression, as many political prisoners were held here during times of uprising.

One of the detainees of the fort is none other than José Rizal — one of the most notable figures in the country's history. Rizal's ideals and literary pieces have been credited for fueling the development of nationalist sentiments among Filipinos at the time. His imprisonment and eventual execution by firing squad have been immortalized in the nation's history.

In this website, our group shares our visit to Fort Santiago and how being there made Rizal's story feel more real and easier to understand — especially his imprisonment and execution.

II. Historical Context

In October 1896, Rizal was detained on suspicion of stirring up a revolt in the Philippines. Although he had always been advocating reforms through peace, not through violence, the Spanish government believed that his writings had somehow inspired the revolutionaries. After his capture, he was taken back to Manila and incarcerated in Fort Santiago on November 3, 1896.

Rizal was held in prison for 56 days until his trial began. He was accused of rebellion, sedition, and conspiracy. The Spaniards argued that his novels, Noli Me Tangere and El Filibusterismo, had revealed the oppressive nature of the colonial government and stirred resistance in the minds of Filipinos. Letters and testimonials from suspected revolutionaries were used as evidence against him, as well as his association with the Katipunan organization.

A Spanish military court composed solely of Spanish officers subjected Rizal to trial on December 26, 1896. Although the prosecution gave him a legal representative who argued that he sought to reform society and not revolt against it, the trial was very brief and considered unfair. Only two days later, Rizal was found guilty and sentenced to death by firing squad.

Such incidents occurred within the wider scope of Spanish colonial governance, characterized by strict regulations, denial of civil rights, and severe punishment for any form of rebellion. Intellectual acts of dissent were viewed as acts of rebellion against the regime. On December 30, 1896, Rizal met his fate in Bagumbayan — now known as Rizal Park.

III. Site Description & Visual Documentation

❧   Rizal's Prison Cell (Museo ni Rizal)

The prison cell in which José Rizal stayed can currently be seen at the Museo ni Rizal. It is not a large area, but rather cramped with stone walls that help create an atmosphere of isolation. Lighting in the cell is scarce, adding to the somber ambience of the place. The interior is not lavishly decorated but consists of some elements reconstructed to reflect the environment in which Rizal found himself.

There prevails a sensation of heaviness when entering the cell due to its isolation from the external world. The atmosphere is quiet, symbolizing a closed environment in which Rizal had to wait for his impending death. The small dimensions of the space add to this impression, giving one a tangible sense of the mental pressure he endured while imprisoned.

❧   Rizal's Footsteps (Path to Execution)

Perhaps one of the most moving elements at Fort Santiago is the set of bronze footsteps laid across the floor. These footsteps indicate the path that José Rizal took when he left the fort on the day of his execution. They are laid in succession towards the gate, leading visitors along a symbolic final walk.

The contrast between the bronze footsteps and the surrounding stone floor highlights the path and serves as a powerful symbol — a representation of Rizal's own steps during the last moments of his life. Visitors who follow these footsteps to the gate are, in a profound way, walking with Rizal toward his fate.

❧   Chapel Cell (Marriage to Josephine Bracken)

Among the various parts of Fort Santiago, the chapel cell is a highly significant area — it was here that José Rizal married Josephine Bracken on the morning of December 30, 1896, just hours before his execution. In terms of appearance, this section is quite simple, yet that simplicity itself reflects both the religious importance and the historical weight of the space.

This area marks a moment when Rizal was able to live an extremely personal and human experience despite the circumstances awaiting him — creating a striking contrast between the intimacy of the private ceremony and the finality of what followed.

IV. Analysis

José Rizal can be seen as a political prisoner because he was jailed for his ideas and writings, not because he fought in a violent way. His novels Noli Me Tangere and El Filibusterismo exposed the problems of colonial governance, which made the Spanish authorities see him as a threat. His imprisonment illustrates that people during that time could not freely express their thoughts.

At the same time, Rizal became a symbol of resistance. Even if he chose a peaceful path, his ideas still influenced many people. His death did not extinguish his impact — instead, it made his message stronger.

Fort Santiago carries two meanings. Before, it was a place of oppression where prisoners were kept and silenced. Today, it is a Shrine of Freedom where people remember history and honor heroes. This transformation shows how the meaning of a place can evolve over time.

Physical places like Fort Santiago help people remember history better because they can actually see and feel it — making it easier to understand compared to reading alone. The site also reminds visitors of the sacrifices that made the present possible. Rizal's story connects deeply to national identity, sacrifice, and patriotism. His life continues to shape how Filipinos understand freedom today.

✦

Reliving Rizal: A Journey Through History

Illustrados · BSPT 1–1 · Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila · A.Y. 2025–2026

Critical Framework

The ACR Framework

✦

Appreciate · Critique · Recommend — A structured analysis of the life, works, and legacy of José Rizal

A

Appreciate Rizal

His Contributions, Heroism, and Enduring Lessons

The works and contributions of José Rizal are profoundly important to the country. His writings helped open the minds of Filipinos and made them more aware of the injustices of their time. His commitment to education and peaceful reform remains deeply relevant today — a model of intellectual courage that continues to inspire generations.

As a group, from Rizal's life, the most important lesson that should guide young Filipinos today is the value of critical thinking and responsible patriotism. Rizal showed that loving one's country is not just about pride, but also about being aware of its problems and taking action to address them. His life teaches us that education, courage, and integrity are powerful tools in creating change.

As students, we believe that Rizal's example reminds us to be active participants in society — to stay informed, speak thoughtfully, and contribute in meaningful ways. By applying these values in our daily lives, we can continue the progress that Rizal envisioned for the nation.

"He who does not know how to look back at where he came from will never get to his destination." — José Rizal
C

Critique Rizal

Constructive Reflections on His Decisions and Limitations

As a group, we think that José Rizal could have explored alternative approaches alongside his strong commitment to peaceful reform. While his writings were powerful in exposing the injustices of the Spanish colonial government, relying mainly on intellectual and literary methods may have limited the immediate impact of his advocacy.

One possible decision he could have made differently was to engage more directly with organized movements within the Philippines, which might have strengthened the connection between his ideas and collective action. We also recognize that there were clear limitations to his methods. His reformist stance depended on the assumption that the colonial government would be willing to listen and implement change — which, historically, was not the case.

Additionally, his influence was initially more accessible to the educated class, which may have slowed the spread of his ideas among the broader population. One criticism, therefore, is that peaceful reform alone may not have been sufficient during that era given how strict the authorities were.

However, we understand that these choices were shaped by the context of his time as well as his personal principles. His preference for peaceful reform reflects a deliberate and principled approach rather than a lack of awareness of other options. His methods, while limited in immediate political effect, produced a lasting cultural and intellectual transformation that ultimately fueled the revolution he never intended to lead.

R

Recommend

Alternative Paths & Modern Applications

As a group, if we were to advise José Rizal during his time, we would suggest complementing his advocacy for peaceful reform with stronger engagement among the Filipino masses. While his writings were influential, building deeper connections with a wider range of people — especially those outside the educated class — could have helped translate his ideas into more unified collective action.

We would also suggest forming stronger alliances with reformists and leaders within the country to create a more coordinated movement for change. A coalition of intellectuals and community leaders working together might have amplified the reach of his message far beyond the printed page.

From Rizal's life, the most important lesson that should guide young Filipinos today is the value of critical thinking and responsible patriotism. Rizal showed that loving one's country is not just about pride, but also about being aware of its problems and taking action to address them. His life teaches us that education, courage, and integrity are powerful tools in creating change.

As students, we believe that Rizal's example reminds us to be active participants in society — to stay informed, speak thoughtfully, and contribute in meaningful ways. For the transformation of the Philippines, there should be more focus on educating the youth about history and encouraging them to be more active in society. Visiting places like Fort Santiago can help people understand the importance of freedom and history. Applying Rizal's values in real life makes us more aware and responsible citizens.

"The youth is the hope of our future." — José Rizal
✦

Reliving Rizal: A Journey Through History

Illustrados · BSPT 1–1 · Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila · A.Y. 2025–2026

The Illustrados

Our Team

✦

BSPT 1–1 · Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila · Academic Year 2025–2026

Torres, Cian Cassidy L.

Most Unforgettable Experience in the Life and Works of Rizal Course

For me, the most unforgettable experience in this whole course was when we studied about the last days of Rizal in Fort Santiago because it really made me feel how sad and heavy his sacrifice was — just so we can have freedom today. The learning that really stuck in my head is that being a hero is not always about fighting with weapons, because Rizal showed that having a strong mind and a good education is also a very powerful tool to change things.

Also, as a PT student, I realized that we need to have that same kind of heart for service like him when we are treating our patients, because helping people is also a way of being a modern hero. That is why I think this subject is important — it is not just about the past, but also about how we can be better people for our country in the future.

"To foretell the destiny of a nation, it is necessary to open the book that tells of her past."
— José Rizal

Dataylo, Kendahl Raine C.

Most Unforgettable Experience in the Life and Works of Rizal Course

The most unforgettable part of the whole thing was really those bronze footsteps that you can see there, because it really makes you think about Rizal and how he was just walking to his death so calmly. It is just so striking because those footsteps really make history feel like it is happening right now — and because of that, I realized that freedom is not actually free at all, because someone had to walk that path and give up everything.

Also, it is so profound when you imagine the silence of that morning and how those stones were the last things his feet touched. Even if it was a long time ago, you can still feel the sadness but also the pride that is there in the ground. Our life today is because of the steps he took back then — we should not forget it so easily, because it matters a lot for all of us.

"Youth is the hope of our future."
— José Rizal

Camacho, Ma. Kristine B.

Most Unforgettable Experience in the Life and Works of Rizal Course

One of my most unforgettable experiences in this course was how our teacher shared his knowledge about the life and history of José Rizal. The way he explained the lessons made everything more fascinating and meaningful. Many of the things we discussed were new to me — especially the deeper details of Rizal's life that I had never encountered before.

What made the experience special is that it did not feel like a typical class discussion. Instead, it felt engaging and insightful, which helped me understand the subject more clearly. This experience made me appreciate history in a different way, showing me that learning becomes more meaningful when it is shared with passion and depth.

"The youth is the hope of our future."
— José Rizal

Amio, Ariane Jean

Most Unforgettable Experience in the Life and Works of Rizal Course

One of my most unforgettable experiences in our Rizal subject was during the midterm exam. I still remember cramming my review that day, but thankfully, I was able to answer many of the questions. What made it even more memorable was during the checking of our papers — since one person's score would reflect the score of the whole class, we were all hoping our blockmates did well.

In the end, we all got a total score of 89, which was such a relief and a proud moment for all of us.

"The things that scare you only make you better and stronger."

Pura, Jairah Nina M.

Most Unforgettable Experience in the Life and Works of Rizal Course

One of the most unforgettable parts of studying this course was understanding the life and sacrifices of Dr. José Rizal, especially through his works like Noli Me Tangere and El Filibusterismo. I realized how many sacrifices he went through in his life and how hardworking he was as a person — constantly studying, writing, and standing up for his beliefs.

What truly stayed with me is how powerful words and ideas can be in shaping a nation. Rizal showed that change doesn't always come from force, but from courage, intelligence, and love for one's country. This course taught me to value our history more deeply and to reflect on how I can contribute, even in small ways, to society today.

"He who does not know how to look back at where he came from will never get to his destination."
— José Rizal

Reliving Rizal: A Journey Through History

Illustrados · BSPT 1–1 · Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila · A.Y. 2025–2026

Historical Profile

Dr. José Rizal

✦

José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda · June 19, 1861 – December 30, 1896

Dr. José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda

José Protasio Rizal
Mercado y Alonso Realonda

Born: June 19, 1861  ·  Calamba, Laguna  ·  Executed: December 30, 1896

José Rizal was born on June 19, 1861, in Calamba, Laguna, in the Philippines, then under Spanish colonial rule. He was the seventh of eleven children of Francisco Rizal Mercado II and Teodora Alonso Realonda — a well-educated woman who served as one of Rizal's greatest intellectual influences.

From his earliest years, Rizal displayed extraordinary intellectual gifts. By the age of three, he had learned the alphabet; by five, he was reading. His mother, despite being briefly imprisoned by Spanish authorities when Rizal was still a child — an injustice that would deeply shape his worldview — gave him his first lessons in reading and life.

As the son of a wealthy family and a member of the Filipino ilustrado class, Rizal had access to educational opportunities that would eventually take him across Europe and shape him into one of the most accomplished intellectuals of the 19th century. He spoke 22 languages and was, by any measure, one of the most cosmopolitan and learned Filipinos of his era.

Early Life

Raised in an ilustrado (educated middle-class) household, Rizal was exposed to the social inequities of colonial life from an early age. His family's own conflicts with the Spanish authorities — including a dispute over their land — gave him direct, personal experience of the injustices his later novels would chronicle.

Rizal's father, Francisco Mercado, owned a farm in Calamba, while his mother, Teodora Alonzo, was a woman of considerable learning and had significant influence over Rizal's early education. His older brother, Paciano Rizal, was also deeply involved in the reform movement and played a crucial role in supporting Rizal's studies and development as a thinker.

He was educated early in Biñan, Laguna, where he attended private school under Maestro Justiniano Cruz before moving to Manila for his higher education. Even as a child, Rizal showed exceptional talent in painting, poetry, and sculpture — gifts that he would develop throughout his life alongside his medical and scientific studies.

Education

Rizal's formal education began at the Ateneo Municipal de Manila, where he excelled in both academics and the arts, graduating with the highest honors at the age of sixteen. He subsequently studied philosophy and letters at the University of Santo Tomás before transferring to pursue medicine in Madrid, earning his Licentiate in Medicine in 1884.

Driven by a desire to cure his mother's failing eyesight, he specialized in ophthalmology and continued his studies in Paris and Heidelberg, Germany — where he worked under the renowned Professor Otto Becker. During his time in Europe, he completed his first novel and became deeply involved with the Propaganda Movement, a reform campaign advocating for Philippine rights.

1869–1872
Biñan, Laguna — Private School
Early formal instruction; demonstrated exceptional aptitude in Latin and Spanish
1872–1877
Ateneo Municipal de Manila
Graduated with sobresaliente (highest honors); developed love of poetry and literature under the Jesuits. Wrote "Sa Aking mga Kabata"
1877–1882
University of Santo Tomás, Manila
Studied philosophy & letters, then pre-medicine; grew frustrated with discrimination against Filipino students; decided to study in Europe
1882–1884
Universidad Central de Madrid, Spain
Earned Licentiate in Medicine (1884) & Licentiate in Philosophy and Letters; active in the Filipino expatriate community and Propaganda Movement
1885
University of Paris, France
Further specialization in ophthalmology; worked under Dr. Louis de Wecker
1886
University of Heidelberg, Germany
Studied under the renowned Professor Otto Becker; completed Noli Me Tangere while here

Major Works

Rizal was a prolific writer, novelist, poet, essayist, and polemicist. His two novels are considered the most important works in Philippine literature, as they directly challenged Spanish colonial rule and awakened nationalist consciousness among Filipinos.

1887
Noli Me Tangere
His first novel, written in Spanish in Berlin. Exposed the abuses of the Spanish clergy and colonial government in the Philippines. The title, Latin for "Touch Me Not," comes from the Gospel of John.
1891
El Filibusterismo
The darker sequel to Noli Me Tangere. Chronicles the return of Crisostomo Ibarra as the revolutionary Simoun, depicting a Philippines on the brink of violent uprising.
1896
Mi Último Adiós
His farewell poem, written the night before his execution. Hidden in a small oil lamp and given to his sister Trinidad, it expresses his profound love for the Philippines and his acceptance of death.
1890
Annotation of Morga's Sucesos
Rizal annotated Antonio de Morga's 1609 history of the Philippines to prove that Filipino civilization existed and thrived before Spanish colonization.
1879
A La Juventud Filipina
Prize-winning poem dedicated to Filipino youth, written at age 18. Proclaimed the youth as the "fair hope of my Fatherland" — one of his earliest nationalist expressions.
1880
El Consejo de los Dioses
An allegorical play that won first prize in a literary contest at the Ateneo. Demonstrated his early mastery of classical literature and drama.

Legacy

In 1896, José Rizal was arrested and imprisoned in Fort Santiago for charges of rebellion, sedition, and conspiracy. He was then subjected to a brief and widely condemned military trial and sentenced to death. Shortly before his execution, Rizal and Josephine Bracken were married in the Chapel Cell at Fort Santiago.

On December 30, 1896, José Rizal was executed by firing squad at Bagumbayan — now Rizal Park — at the age of 35. Moments before his death, he turned to face the rising sun rather than the firing squad, a final act of defiance and dignity. His execution, rather than silencing the nationalist movement, galvanized the Philippine Revolution and ultimately led to independence.

Today, Rizal is recognized as the National Hero of the Philippines. December 30 is observed as Rizal Day, a national holiday. His image appears on the Philippine one-peso coin and the twenty-peso bill. Schools, parks, streets, provinces, and monuments throughout the archipelago bear his name. His novels remain required reading in Philippine schools, and his life continues to serve as a model of intellectual courage, patriotism, and sacrifice.

"I die without seeing the dawn brighten over my native land. You who have it to see, welcome it — and forget not those who have fallen during the night." — José Rizal, Mi Último Adiós (1896)
✦

Reliving Rizal: A Journey Through History

Illustrados · BSPT 1–1 · Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila · A.Y. 2025–2026

Life & Legacy

Timeline of José Rizal

✦

From Calamba to Bagumbayan — The life, works, and final days of the National Hero

June 19, 1861
Birth in Calamba, Laguna
José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda is born in Calamba, Laguna — the seventh of eleven children of Francisco Mercado II and Teodora Alonso Realonda, under Spanish colonial rule.
1869–1872
Early Schooling in Biñan
Rizal attends private school under Maestro Justiniano Cruz, displaying exceptional early aptitude in Latin, Spanish, and painting.
1872
Mother's Imprisonment
Teodora Alonso, Rizal's mother, is imprisoned by Spanish authorities on trumped-up charges. This injustice profoundly shapes young Rizal's worldview and fuels his future advocacy.
1872–1877
Ateneo Municipal de Manila
Rizal excels in academics and the arts under the Jesuits, graduating with the highest honors (sobresaliente) at age 16. He writes "Sa Aking mga Kabata," an early poem on love of the native language.
1879
"A La Juventud Filipina" — Prize-Winning Poem
Rizal's poem "A La Juventud Filipina" wins first prize in a literary contest organized by the Liceo Artístico-Literario. He dedicates the poem to Filipino youth, calling them the hope of the Fatherland.
1877–1882
University of Santo Tomás
Studies philosophy and letters, then pre-medicine. Grows increasingly frustrated with the discrimination against Filipino students under Dominican professors. Decides to continue his studies in Europe.
1882–1884
Universidad Central de Madrid — Medicine
Earns his Licentiate in Medicine in 1884 and his Licentiate in Philosophy and Letters. Becomes active in the Filipino community in Madrid and joins the Propaganda Movement, writing essays for La Solidaridad.
1885–1886
Ophthalmology in Paris & Heidelberg
Driven by a desire to cure his mother's failing eyesight, Rizal specializes in ophthalmology in Paris under Dr. Louis de Wecker, then in Heidelberg, Germany under the renowned Professor Otto Becker.
1887
Publication of Noli Me Tangere
Rizal publishes his landmark novel in Berlin, Germany — a scathing portrayal of Spanish colonial abuses in the Philippines. The novel sends shockwaves through the colonial establishment and makes Rizal a marked man.
1890
Annotation of Morga's Sucesos de las Islas Filipinas
Rizal annotates the 1609 historical work by Antonio de Morga to demonstrate that Filipino civilization flourished before Spanish colonization — a powerful act of historical reclamation.
1891
Publication of El Filibusterismo
The darker, more radical sequel to Noli Me Tangere is published in Ghent, Belgium. The novel depicts a Philippines on the edge of revolution and further inflames colonial authorities.
1892
Return to the Philippines — La Liga Filipina — Exile to Dapitan
Rizal returns to the Philippines and founds La Liga Filipina, a civic organization advocating peaceful reform. He is arrested days later and exiled to Dapitan, Mindanao for four years, where he practices medicine and teaches the local community.
1896
The Katipunan Revolution Breaks Out
The Katipunan, a secret revolutionary society, launches an armed revolution against Spain under Andres Bonifacio. Although Rizal had no involvement in the Katipunan, Spanish authorities use the outbreak to target him.
October 1896
Arrest and Return to Manila
Rizal is arrested aboard a ship in Barcelona, Spain, on his way to Cuba as a military physician. He is brought back to Manila by Spanish authorities on suspicion of inspiring the revolution through his writings.
November 3, 1896
Imprisoned in Fort Santiago
Rizal is incarcerated in Fort Santiago, Intramuros, Manila. He is held in a cramped prison cell for 56 days awaiting trial on charges of rebellion, sedition, and conspiracy against the Spanish Crown.
December 26, 1896
Trial by Military Court
A Spanish military court composed solely of Spanish officers subjects Rizal to trial. His defense attorney argues that he sought only peaceful reform, not revolution. The proceedings are widely condemned as a show trial.
December 28, 1896
Sentenced to Death
Rizal is found guilty and sentenced to death by firing squad. On the night before his execution, he writes his farewell poem "Mi Último Adiós" and hides it in a small oil lamp, which he gives to his sister Trinidad.
December 30, 1896 — Morning
Marriage to Josephine Bracken
In the Chapel Cell at Fort Santiago, Rizal and his companion Josephine Bracken are married in a brief Catholic ceremony — a final, profoundly human act of love in the hours before his death.
December 30, 1896 — 7:03 A.M.
Execution at Bagumbayan
José Rizal is executed by firing squad at Bagumbayan (now Rizal Park), Manila. He turns to face the rising sun in a final act of defiance. He is 35 years old. His death ignites the Philippine Revolution and sets the nation on its path to independence.
1901 onward
National Hero — Enduring Legacy
José Rizal is officially recognized as the National Hero of the Philippines. December 30 is declared Rizal Day, a national holiday. His novels remain required reading in Philippine schools, and his life continues to inspire generations of Filipinos.
✦

In honor of José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda · December 30, 1896
